
# Wrangling {.unnumbered}

![](images/wrangling.png){width="650" fig-align="center"} 

<p font-size=55pt align="center">
**Data wrangling transforms raw data into a structured format.** 
</p>

The process includes collecting, tidying, and preprocessing raw data so that it can be easily analyzed and visualised. We will use ```pandas``` for these tasks.

## Reading

::: {.callout-tip}
### What types of data files do you generate in your research?
:::


First, we need to access our data and store it in a variable.

Pandas has a function that allows us to *read in* an excel document: ```read_excel()```. 

```{python}
#| echo: True
#| output-location: fragment
# import pandas, aliased as pd
import pandas as pd

# Import an Excel file called "example" from the data folder
df = pd.read_excel('data/example_proteins.xlsx', sheet_name='Sheet1')

# Visualise the first few rows
print(df.head(2))
```

```{python}
#| echo: True
# Visualise the first few rows
df.head(2)
```

Pandas has many other ```read``` functions for all types of data.

![](images/pandas_read.png){width="700" fig-align="center"} 

```{python}
#| echo: True
# From a plain text document:
df = pd.read_table('data/example_proteins.txt', delimiter='\t')

# From a csv document:
df = pd.read_csv('data/example_proteins.csv')
```

## Cleaning columns

Cleaning standardises and simplifies a dataset.

This might involve adjusting column names, correcting data types, and separating pieces of information from one column into many.

We can access the column names for a dataframe using ```.columns```.

```{python}
#| echo: False
#| output: False
df = pd.read_excel('data/example_proteins.xlsx', sheet_name='Sheet1')
```

```{python}
#| echo: True
print(df.columns)
```

We can drop columns by name using the ```.drop()``` method.

```{python}
#| echo: True
df.drop('Subcellular Location', axis=1)
df.head(2)
```

We can access the column names for a dataframe using ```.columns```.

```{python}
#| echo: False
#| output: False
df = pd.read_excel('data/example_proteins.xlsx', sheet_name='Sheet1')
```

```{python}
#| echo: True
print(df.columns)
```

We can drop columns by name using the ```.drop()``` method.

```{python}
#| echo: True
df.drop('Subcellular Location', axis=1, inplace=True)
df.head(2)
```
::: {.callout-note}
### Explore other methods available for DataFrame operations [here](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.html#:~:text=of%20the%20DataFrame.-,Methods,-abs()).
:::

We can update the column names for a dataframe by directly updating the ```.columns```, or using the ```.rename()``` method.

```{python}
#| echo: True
df.columns = ['protein', 'molecular_weight_kda', 'function']
df.head(2)
```

We can update the column names for a dataframe by directly updating the ```.columns```, or using the ```.rename()``` method.

```{python}
#| echo: True
df.rename(columns={
  'Protein': 'protein', 
  'Molecular Weight (kDa)': 'molecular_weight_kda', 
  'Function': 'function'}, inplace=True)
       
df.head(2)
```

::: {.callout-note}
### Adopt variable naming conventions (all lowercase characters, separated by underscores) to make column names consistent and easy to access.
:::


To select individual columns, we can then use ```[colname]``` or ```.colname```.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
:::{.fragment}
```{python}
#| echo: True
df['protein']
```
:::
:::

::: {.column}
:::{.fragment}
```{python}
#| echo: True
df.protein
```
:::
:::
:::

:::{.fragment}
::: {.callout-important}
### The ```.``` notation only works when the column names do not have spaces.
:::
:::

```{python}
#| echo: False
#| output: False
df = pd.read_csv('data/example_proteins.csv')
df.drop([col for col in df.columns.tolist() if 'Unnamed: ' in col], axis=1, inplace=True)
df.rename(columns={
  'Protein': 'protein', 
  'Molecular Weight (kDa)': 'molecular_weight_kda', 
  'Subcellular Location': 'subcellular_location', 
  'log(2)_expression': 'expression_log2', 
  'Time point (h)': 'time_h', 
  'Donor': 'donor_id', 
  'Function': 'function'}, inplace=True)
df['molecular_weight_kda'] = df['molecular_weight_kda'].astype(str)

```

## Cleaning data types

The ```dtype``` of each column dictates what operations we can do with it's data. 

```{python}
#| echo: True
print(df.dtypes)
```

We can convert between data types using the ```.astype()``` method.

```{python}
#| echo: True
#| output-location: fragment
df['molecular_weight_kda'].astype(float)

print(df.dtypes)
```

The ```dtype``` of each column dictates what operations we can do with it's data. 

```{python}
#| echo: True
print(df.dtypes)
```

We can convert between data types using the ```.astype()``` method.

```{python}
#| echo: True
df['molecular_weight_kda'] = df['molecular_weight_kda'].astype(float)

print(df.dtypes)
```

Often string data are composites joined by one or more *delimiters*. To use these data elements we need to separate elements into different columns using the ```.str``` and ```.split``` methods.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}
```{python}
#| echo: True
df['protein']
```
:::

::: {.column}
```{python}
#| echo: True
df['protein'].str.split('_')
```
:::
:::

## Cleaning composite data

Often string data are composites joined by one or more *delimiters*. To use these data elements we need to separate elements into different columns using the ```.str``` and ```.split``` methods.

```{python}
#| echo: True
# Assign split strings to separate columns using 'expand' keyword
df[['protein_name', 'gene']] = df['protein'].str.split('_', expand=True)

df.head(2)
```

```{python}
df.to_csv(f'data/clean_data.csv')
```

::: {.callout-note}

### These operations will be very dataset specific, and each dataset will likely require a combination of approaches.
:::


## In practice

::: {.callout-caution}
### Exercises

3.1 Reading and cleaning

:::
